topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed a monotonous, irritating tune that seemed to vibrate directly behind my eyes. I stared at the meticulously formatted bibliography, the words blurring into meaningless ink. Professor Albright’s departure had been announced with the casual finality of a scheduled maintenance update—efficient, impersonal, and utterly devastating to my carefully constructed timeline. I traced the spine of a heavy textbook, the leather cool beneath my fingertips, and felt a profound, heavy slump settle into my shoulders. Every time I tried to pull myself back to the complex statistical models, the effort felt monumental, like trying to lift a boulder with my bare hands. I sighed, a shallow, almost inaudible expulsion of air, and closed the laptop lid, the resulting silence amplifying the frantic drumming in my chest.

[story 2]

"So, that's it then," I mumbled, the words tasting like dust in my mouth. The air in the office felt suddenly thin, stripped of the familiar, comforting presence of Professor Albright’s chaotic energy. I picked at a loose thread on my sweater, my gaze fixed on the window where the afternoon sun cast long, indifferent shadows across the quad. My hands, resting on the desk, began to tremble slightly, a subtle, uncontrollable vibration that had nothing to do with caffeine withdrawal. I reread the last email—a brief note about transition plans—and the sheer emptiness of the space where guidance used to reside pressed down on me. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal against the linoleum sounding deafeningly loud in the quiet room.

[story 3]

I spent an hour just staring at the condensation collecting on my water bottle, watching the droplets slowly merge and slide down the plastic. The weight of the impending void—the sudden absence of the person who had been the anchor for this entire project—was settling deep in my core, making simple tasks feel like climbing a sheer cliff face. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the ache radiating up into my temples. When I finally forced myself to open the research document again, the text swam. I minimized the file, letting the screen go dark, and leaned my head against the cool surface of the desk. For a long moment, I didn't move, just letting the stillness envelop me, a complete surrender to the exhaustion that clung to my limbs like damp fabric.
